name: Passadiço do Gameiro — circular route in Cabeção de Portalegre
name_original: Passadiço do Gameiro — percurso circular em Cabeção de Portalegre
region_intro: Family friendly hiking trails in Évora traverse a landscape characterized by rolling plains, extensive cork oak forests known as "montados," and ancient olive groves. The region is also notable for its significant prehistoric monuments, including megalithic sites that offer unique historical exploration alongside natural beauty. Hikers can explore trails that follow parts of the 16th-century Aqueduto da Água de Prata, providing views of the city and surrounding countryside. This diverse terrain offers a blend of cultural heritage and natural features for outdoor enthusiasts. Best family friendly hiking trails in Évora The most popular family friendly hiking route is Escritas de Pedra e Cal (PR 1) - circular route, a 7.6 miles (12.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 22 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers a comprehensive experience of the local landscape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Monsaraz Menhirs and Churches Route, a moderate 8.8 miles (14.1 km) path. This trail combines exploration of ancient megalithic sites with views of a medieval village. Local hikers also love the Passadiço do Gameiro — circular route in Cabeção de Portalegre, a 3.7 miles (5.9 km) trail leading through fields and quiet forests, often completed in about 1 hour 36 minutes. Hiking in Évora is defined by cork oak forests, rolling plains, and prehistoric monuments, with routes offering options for easy family walks to more moderate excursions. The routes in Évora are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 300 reviews. More than 1,400 hikers have used komoot to explore Évora's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many family-friendly hiking trails can I find around Évora?
A: Based on komoot data, there are over 70 family-friendly hiking routes in the Évora region, with 42 of them rated as easy, making them suitable for various ages and fitness levels.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about family-friendly trails in Évora?
A: The komoot community rates the family-friendly trails in Évora highly, with an average score of 4.48 out of 5 stars. Hikers often praise the region's unique blend of historical sites, such as ancient megaliths, and the serene natural landscapes, including cork oak forests and rolling plains, which offer engaging experiences for all ages.

Q: Are there any family-friendly circular routes in Évora?
A: Yes, Évora offers several family-friendly circular routes. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2245478346/passadico-do-gameiro-circular-route-in-cabecao-de-portalegre" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Passadiço do Gameiro — circular route in Cabeção de Portalegre&lt;/a&gt;, which is rated as easy and covers nearly 6 km, perfect for a pleasant family outing. Another moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2260935516/escritas-de-pedra-e-cal-pr-1-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Escritas de Pedra e Cal (PR 1) - circular route&lt;/a&gt;, offering a longer adventure.

Q: What kind of natural features and landmarks can we expect to see on family hikes in Évora?
A: Évora's family-friendly hikes often lead through diverse landscapes. You can explore ancient cork oak forests, vast plains, and olive groves. Many trails also feature impressive historical landmarks, such as the megalithic sites like Almendres Cromlech and Anta Grande do Zambujeiro. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14655697" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almendres Menhir – Almendres Cromlech loop from Guadalupe&lt;/a&gt; takes you directly to these prehistoric monuments.

Q: Are there any trails suitable for strollers or very young children?
A: While many trails in Évora feature natural terrain, the Ecopista, a converted railway track, offers a mostly flat and accessible route ideal for strollers and very young children. For specific routes, look for trails rated as 'easy' with minimal elevation gain, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2245478346/passadico-do-gameiro-circular-route-in-cabecao-de-portalegre" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Passadiço do Gameiro&lt;/a&gt;, which is generally well-maintained.

Q: Can we bring our dog on the family hiking trails in Évora?
A: Many outdoor areas and trails in Évora are dog-friendly, especially in the natural countryside. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially near livestock or protected areas, and to check specific regulations for any nature reserves or private properties you might encounter. Always carry water for your pet and clean up after them.

Q: What is the best time of year for family hiking in Évora?
A: The best time for family hiking in Évora is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the occasional heavy rains of winter. Spring also brings blooming wildflowers, while autumn offers beautiful colors in the landscape.

Q: Are there any castles or historical villages accessible via family-friendly hikes?
A: Yes, Évora's region is rich in history. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2255850189/monsaraz-menhirs-and-churches-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monsaraz Menhirs and Churches Route&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, combines megalithic sites with the charming medieval village of Monsaraz. You can also find highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/612540" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monsaraz Castle&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6260983" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monsaraz Historic Village&lt;/a&gt; nearby, offering a blend of nature and culture for families.

Q: Are there any trails that offer shade, especially during warmer days?
A: Yes, many trails in Évora pass through the region's characteristic cork oak and holm oak forests, known as 'montados.' These forests provide natural shade, offering a cooler hiking experience, especially during sunnier days. Look for routes that specifically mention passing through wooded areas or forests for more shaded options.

Q: Do I need any permits to hike the family-friendly trails in Évora?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for hiking on public trails in the Évora region. However, if you plan to visit specific nature reserves or private estates, it's always advisable to check local regulations beforehand. Most family-friendly routes are freely accessible.

Q: Are there places to eat or get refreshments near the trailheads?
A: While some trailheads might be more remote, many family-friendly routes are located near or pass through smaller towns and villages where you can find local cafes and restaurants. For example, routes near Monsaraz or those starting from villages often have amenities. It's always a good idea to pack snacks and water, especially for longer hikes.

Q: What kind of terrain should we expect on family hikes in Évora?
A: The terrain on family hikes in Évora is generally varied, ranging from flat, well-maintained paths like the Ecopista to more natural dirt tracks through rolling plains, cork oak forests, and olive groves. Some trails might have gentle inclines, but most easy and moderate family routes avoid steep or challenging ascents, making them suitable for children.
tips:
The Gameiro Ecological Park was created at the Gameiro Dam, on the Raia River. It is a park that serves as a point of interest for the municipality, as it has a diverse range of attractions and infrastructures available. Examples include the Camping Park, Picnic Park, Leisure Area, Mora River and the Tree Climbing Park in the surrounding area, designed for extreme sports, and the Wooden Walkway along 1.5 km of the Raia River. 
The Gameiro Ecological Park is located in the parish of Cabeção, in the municipality of Mora.

The walkway begins at the charming Praia Fluvial do Gameiro and immediately plunges you into a wilder Alentejo, always following the Raia river. As you progress, the montados embrace the trail and the sound of the water soothes the landscape.

Here, amidst the shadows and silence, it is not uncommon to come across grey herons, kingfishers or jays, winged sentinels of this riverside paradise.

The Ribeira da Raia is a Portuguese watercourse that originates in the Alentejo, in the parish of Cabeção, from the junction of the Seda and Tera rivers. 

It passes north of the town of Mora and flows into the left bank of the Ribeira de Sor, in the parish of Couço, where they form the Sorraia river. 

The name Raia probably has Arabic origins and designates the border region of the Almohad territory, just like the place name Soutelinho da Raia on the border between Portugal and Spain.
